column1: # AccuracySNES rendered-scene goldens (docs/adr/0013).
# Format: <scene id>\t<hash>
# The hash is FNV-1a over a fixed 256x224 region of canonical 0RRRRRGGGGGBBBBB pixels. Fixed and
# canonical because emulators agree about neither geometry nor pixel format, and a golden has to
# compare pictures rather than output conventions. Each host declares where its own picture starts
# in its own buffer (`FIRST_ROW`): RustySNES 0, snes9x's libretro core 0, Mesen2 7.
# ADR 0013 rule 4: a golden may be blessed ONLY from a cross-validated render -- a scene the
# reference emulators have been shown to agree on, with the agreement recorded here. A scene with
# no entry is reported as unblessed and does not fail the gate, because an unblessed scene is not
# yet evidence of anything.
# Separate from undisbeliever-framebuffer.tsv on purpose: these are first-party and may be
# regenerated when WE intend a behaviour change; those are third-party and may not.
# Cross-validated three ways on the identical image, all agreeing bit-for-bit:
#   * RustySNES         crates/rustysnes-test-harness/tests/accuracysnes_scenes.rs
#   * snes9x (libretro) scripts/accuracysnes/libretro_crossval.c --scenes
#   * Mesen2 (headless) scripts/accuracysnes/mesen_scenes.lua
# Two pairs (three scenes) are deliberately expected to hash IDENTICALLY, and the harness checks
# that in its own test rather than only checking each against its own number. An equivalence is the
# stronger statement: it survives a change to the canvas, and it fails for a core that gets both
# scenes wrong in the same way, which matching a committed number does not.
#   c8-half-ignored-on-fixed-backdrop == c8-fixed-colour-add
#       CGADSUB's half/div2 bit is ignored when the subscreen is the fixed backdrop (C8.03).
#   c8-window-left-gt-right-empty == c8-both-windows-disabled-empty
#       Crossed window bounds and no enabled window are both EMPTY masks, not full ones
#       (C8.05, C8.07) -- so BG1 is fully visible in both.
#   c5-mode7-ignores-bgsc == c11-mode7-identity
#       Mode 7 has its own fixed VRAM layout and reads neither BG1SC nor BG1NBA, so pointing both
#       at nonsense changes nothing (C5.13).
# 2026-07-20, first batch (3 scenes). All three disagreed with the references, and all three were
# RustySNES bugs: the BG vertical fetch was a line late, and mosaic quantised the BG's own row
# rather than the screen row. Agreement with snes9x across the 29-ROM undisbeliever suite went from
# 2/29 to 14/29 once both were fixed.
# 2026-07-20, second batch (15 scenes). No divergence -- expected, since both fixed bugs sit
# upstream of most of what those scenes render.
# 2026-07-20, third batch (7 scenes: offset-per-tile and a mode-2 control). No divergence. The two
# deep-colour scenes from the second batch were RE-BLESSED here because `scene_low_tiles` changed:
# it now picks tiles $10-$1F (printable at 4bpp and 8bpp alike) and varies the tile with the row.
# Both changes were forced by scenes that rendered a picture no assertion could show --
# see the plan doc. The re-bless is a deliberate change to the input, not drift in the output.
# 2026-07-20, fourth batch (10 scenes: Mode 7). THREE more RustySNES bugs, all confirmed against
# both references agreeing with each other:
#   * the Mode 7 vertical fetch was a line late -- the identical off-by-one the first batch found
#     in the tiled backgrounds, missed because `render_mode7` is a separate function. Nine of the
#     ten scenes moved on that one line.
#   * EXTBG replaced BG1 instead of adding a layer, so enabling it made BG1 vanish entirely.
#   * Mode 7 had no mosaic handling at all, and rendered identically with and without it.
#   Re-blessed within the same batch after review: the Mode 7 character byte is now the word index
#   PLUS ONE, so character 0's pixel (0,0) is non-zero. At a large zoom every out-of-range pixel
#   samples that one sub-pixel, so a zero there made the char-0 screen-over mode render transparent
#   and hash identically to the transparent mode -- on all three emulators, which is exactly the
#   kind of agreement that proves nothing.
# 2026-07-20, fifth batch (3 scenes: the scroll-register write-twice latch, C4). No divergence.
# The write-twice latch is shared across all four backgrounds AND both axes, which is only visible
# when a register is written ONCE and the next write goes somewhere else -- so `c4-shared-scroll-
# latch` writes one byte to $210D and one to $210E and reads the result off the V scroll.
# 2026-07-20, sixth batch (3 scenes: sprites, C7). No divergence. OAM is 544 bytes of state that
# nothing else clears, so `scene_oam_reset` parks all 128 sprites at Y=224 first -- a sprite scene
# otherwise renders its own sprites PLUS whatever the previous scene left behind.
# 2026-07-20, seventh batch (2 new scenes + 1 RE-BLESSED, C7 sprite sizes). ONE RustySNES bug,
# found by correcting a scene rather than by adding one:
#   * `c7-vflip-tall-halves` was selecting OBJSEL pair 3 with the sprite's size bit set, which is
#     32x32 -- a SQUARE sprite, on which C7.13's errata says nothing. Only pairs 6 and 7 have a
#     non-square member. Pointed at a real 16x32 sprite, the scene immediately split three ways:
#     snes9x and Mesen2 agreed and RustySNES did not.
#   * The bug: vertical flip was computed against the sprite's HEIGHT. Hardware computes it against
#     its WIDTH, so each square half of a rectangular sprite flips inside itself and the halves do
#     not swap places. Identical for square sprites, which is why nothing else moved. RustySNES now
#     matches both references bit-for-bit, and the golden below is that agreed value.
#   * The two new scenes cover the undocumented size pairs themselves (`C7.10`), and differ from
#     each other by one OBJSEL bit -- the large member's height, 64 against 32.
# 2026-07-20, eighth batch (1 scene: sprite colour math, C8.01). No divergence. Two identical
# sprites in palettes 2 and 6 with colour math enabled for OBJ: only the palette-6 one blends,
# because sprite colour math applies to palettes 4-7 and to nothing else. All three emulators
# already agreed, which is the outcome to expect from an errata everyone has implemented -- the
# scene's value is that the next core to be measured against this corpus cannot skip it.
# 2026-07-20, ninth batch (1 scene: direct colour, C12.02). No divergence. Pixel value 0 is
# transparent in every mode, direct colour included, so pure black is not reachable through it and
# the backdrop shows instead.
# A second scene was attempted in the same batch for C11.02 (Mode 7's 13-bit origin mask) and was
# NOT blessed then: it rendered a picture identical to `c11-mode7-identity`, on all three
# emulators. $1C00 is 7 * $400, so the mask only clears multiples of 1024 -- and a Mode 7 map is
# 1024x1024 and wraps, so every value the mask removes is one the wrap removes anyway. A working
# version landed in the tenth batch below; the golden at the bottom of this file is that one.
# 2026-07-20, tenth batch (1 scene: C11.02 again, now working). Screen-over set to TRANSPARENT is
# what makes the mask visible: the masked origin (64) is on the map and the unmasked one (3136) is
# not, so the two possibilities are a picture and a blank screen. Two dead ends were needed to get
# there, both caught by a hash colliding with an existing scene's:
#   * screen-over left at wrap -- identical to `c11-mode7-identity`, because the mask only removes
#     multiples of 1024 and wrapping removes those anyway;
#   * M7HOFS = $1C40 -- identical to `c8-window-inverted-empty-is-full`, i.e. all backdrop, because
#     M7HOFS is thirteen bits SIGNED and $1C40 is negative. The origin is off the map to the left
#     either way and the mask changes nothing. $0C40 is the same low bits with bit 12 clear.
# A duplicate hash is the only thing that caught either. Checking a new scene's hash against the
# committed goldens is worth doing every time.
# 2026-07-20, eleventh batch (1 scene: C5.13, an EQUIVALENCE). Mode 7 ignores BG1SC and BG1NBA, so
# the scene sets both to nonsense and must render exactly what `c11-mode7-identity` renders. Its
# hash duplicating another is the assertion here rather than a warning -- which is the difference
# between a declared equivalence and the accidental collisions above. An equivalence also survives
# a change to the shared Mode 7 canvas, where a second committed hash would not.
# 2026-07-20, twelfth batch (1 scene: Mode 4, C5.05). No divergence. BG1 8bpp and BG2 2bpp from the
# same tilemap, three bits of colour depth apart, with BG3's offset table left inert.
# A C5.12 scene (a 64x32 map scrolled 256 px into its second screen) was attempted and NOT blessed:
# it rendered the plain canvas, hash-identical to `c8-window-left-gt-right-empty`. The canvas's
# tilemap repeats horizontally with a period dividing 256, so the scroll is invisible whichever
# screen it lands in. It needs a marker written into the second screen first -- see the plan doc.
# 2026-07-20, thirteenth batch (1 scene: C5.12, the map-size placement). No divergence. The
# `scene_second_screen` helper the twelfth batch called for now fills the screen after the canvas
# with tiles $20-$2F at a flat palette 5, varying with the column and nothing else. The canvas
# draws 64 glyphs from $21 upward with a row-derived palette, so the tile numbers overlap and the
# two are still nothing alike as pictures -- scrolling 256 pixels into a 64x32 map's second screen
# renders something no other scene renders.
# 2026-07-20, fourteenth batch (1 scene: C7.11, sprite name-select). No divergence. Two sprites
# with the same tile number, one with the name-select bit set: the second reads $1000 words on,
# where the font never reached, and draws nothing.
# A C7.12 scene (a 16x32 sprite under OBJ interlace) was attempted and produced THREE DIFFERENT
# hashes on three emulators -- the only three-way split any scene has produced. Not a disagreement
# about interlace: the picture alternates every frame, and the capture protocol does not pin frame
# parity, so each host lands on whichever field its own counter happens to be on. See the plan doc.
# Two bugs in the scene before it measured anything, both mine: the first version had no marker
# (the canvas repeats every 256 px, so the scroll was invisible), and the second wrote the scroll
# to $210F, which is BG2HOFS. BG1HOFS is $210D. Each produced a stable, three-way-agreed hash that
# was identical to an existing scene's.
